rulings.Case Title: ANNA MAR VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn For Preliminary Injunctive Relief Or For Stay
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, July 23, 2019, Line 1, PETITIONER ANNA MAR's Motion For Preliminary Injunctive Relief Or For Stay. Petitioner Anna Mar's motion for preliminary injunction or stay is denied. Petitioner does not demonstrate a likelihood of prevailing on the merits or irreparable harm. Petitioner fails to show that Respondent's refusal to grant discretionary review of the Rasmusson's building permit was an abuse of discretion. Respondent has broad discretion in determining whether to grant discretionary review. (See Guinnane v. San Francisco City Planning Com., (1989) 209 Cal.App.3d 732, 740; Ideal Boat & Camper Storage v. City of Alameda (2012) 208 Cal.App.4th 301, 317.) Respondent's guidelines provide that discretionary review is appropriate in "exceptional and extraordinary circumstances." (Petitioner's Ex Parte Application, Ex. 6-3, pg. 2.) Such circumstances do not exist in this case. The overwhelming evidence in the record, i.e., the Shea Report, establishes that the Rasmusson's renovations will not meaningfully harm Petitioner's tree. Respondent did not abuse its discretion or neglect to perform a mandatory duty. Public Resources Code § 21000 and San Francisco Planning Code § 101 make broad pronouncements about protecting the general welfare and the environment. Such amorphous provisions do not clearly define the specific duties or course of conduct that respondent must take and therefore, they are not the proper prerequisite for a traditional mandamus action. (See Schwartz v. Poizner (2010) 187 Cal.App.4th 592, 596-597 [describing ministerial act]; Morris v. Harper (2001) 94 Cal.App.4th 52, 58 ["mandate will lie to compel performance of a clear, present, and usually ministerial duty"]; Bullis Charter School v. Los Altos School Dist. (2011) 200 Cal.App.4th 1022, 1035 [mandamus applies where the statute "clearly defines the specific duties or course of conduct"].) Read together, Respondent's Discretionary Review Action DRA-0598, Discretionary Review Abbreviated Analysis, and the Board of Appeal's Final Decision, make it clear that Petitioner's appeal was denied because the permit was properly issued and there were no extraordinary circumstances to warrant discretionary review. Respondent's decision complies with Topanga Ass'n for a Scenic Community v. City & County of Los Angeles (1974) 11 Cal.3d 506, 515. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Counsel for Respondent is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: HOEM & ASSOCIATES INC., A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION VS. UNIVERSITY OF THE PACIFIC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Reopen Limited Discovery Due To Necessity Of Trial Continuance
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Tuesday, July 23, 2019, Line 3, Motion To Reopen Limited Discovery Due To Necessity Of Trial Continuance. Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. Cross-Defendant and Cross Complainant's motion to reopen discovery for preparation of its newly retained architect expert is granted. At the hearing Hoem shall detail for discussion and review the specific discovery it seeks to accomplish that result. The Court denies the request for global reopening of discovery and a cut-off consistent with the new trial date. For all non-expert preparation specific discovery sought in the motion the parties shall address application of the criteria set forth in Code of Civil Procedure Section 2024.050(b). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@sbslawsf.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ERIC SMITH VS. BEST BUY CO., INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION Of Issue On Cross-Complaint Against Dji Technology, Inc.
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, July 23, 2019, Line 6, DEFENDANT BEST BUY STORES, LP's MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION Of Issue On Cross-Complaint Against DJI Technology, Inc. [Part 1 of 2]. Defendant/Cross-Complainant Best Buy's motion for summary adjudication is granted. DJI Technology, Inc. owes an immediate duty to defend Eric Smith's complaint. There is no dispute that the parties entered into the Vendor Master Agreement that incorporated the indemnification covenant. Best Buy's failure to submit a signed copy of the agreement does not show a lack of mutual consent. The Vendor Master Agreement does not include language that it would become operative only when signed by the parties. "The cases are legion to the effect that when the respective parties orally agree upon all of the terms and conditions of an agreement with the mutual intention that it shall thereupon become binding, the mere fact that a formal written agreement to the same effect is to be prepared and signed does not alter the binding validity of the oral agreement." (Columbia Pictures Corp. v. De Toth (1948) 87 Cal.App.2d 620, 629.) An agreement can be enforceable where it lacks a signature. (See Vita Planning and Landscape Architecture, Inc. v. HKS Architects, Inc. (2015) 240 Cal.App.4th 763, 773 ["The absence of signatures does not render the Contract unenforceable"].) The evidence in this case establishes that the parties entered into the Vendor Master Agreement and DJI does not adduce evidence creating a genuine dispute of material fact. DJI's employee admits that he negotiated the Vendor Master Agreement and the parties are bound by Best Buy's standard terms, which include the indemnification clause. (Shen Depo., 24:19-25; 34:23-35:11.) It is further undisputed that Best Buy sold DJI's products and "[a] voluntary acceptance of the benefit of a transaction is equivalent to a consent to all the obligations arising from it, so far as the facts are known, or ought to be known, to the person accepting." (Civil Code § 1589; see also Architecture, Inc., 240 Cal.App.4th at 773.) (Best Buy's UMF 5-6.) DJI's argument that it does not have an immediate duty to defend Best Buy because the language of the indemnity provision differs from that in Crawford v. Weather Shield Mfg., Inc. (2008) 44 Cal.4th 541 is meritless. The indemnification agreement clearly sets forth an immediate duty to defend. The clause expressly states that "[v]endor will . . . defend . . . . Best Buy"; it requires Best Buy to promptly notify DJI of any claims and to "tender the defense to Vendor"; and it mandates that DJI "pay all costs of . . . defense, including by "us[ing] counsel reasonably satisfactory to Best Buy." Moreover, as Best Buy correctly observes, Crawford held that the obligations set forth in Civil Code Section 2778 "are deemed included in every indemnity agreement unless the parties indicate otherwise." (Id. at 553.) Section 2278(4) explicitly states that the indemnitor "is bound, on request of the person indemnified, to defend actions or proceedings brought against the latter in respect to the matters embraced by the indemnity." This provision "places in every indemnity contract, unless the agreement provides otherwise, a duty to assume the indemnitee's defense, if tendered, against all claims 'embraced by the indemnity.'" (Crawford, 44 Cal.4th at 558.) This "duty arises immediately upon a proper tender of defense by the indemnitee, and thus before the litigation to be defended has determined whether indemnity is actually owed." (Id. (emphasis added).) [Continued to Part 2 of 2]. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: MOAB INVESTMENT GROUP, LLC VS. COURTHOUSE VENTURES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Further Responses To Form Interrogatories And Imposition Of Sanctions Against Defendants And Their Counsel
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Tuesday, July 23, 2019, Line 1,PLAINTIFF MOAB INVESTMENT GROUP, LLC'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Form Interrogatories And Imposition Of Sanctions Against Defendants And Their Counsel. Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff's motion to compel is off calendar without prejudice to refiling within 45 days from appearance of plaintiff's substitute counsel. The moving party is a corporation required to be represented to prosecute a claim. See: Paradise v. Nowlin, 86 Cal. App. 2d 897 (1948). Plaintiff's counsel Kenneth Sperandio's motion to withdraw was granted on June 27, 2019. The Order further provided plaintiff shall have thirty days to retain substitute counsel, which has not occurred to date. Under the circumstances there is no reason for the motion to remain on the Discovery Calendar. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@sbslawsf.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: KENT MAGNELL VS. ARROYO REALTY AND EDEVELOPMENT LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, July 23, 2019, Line 9, DEFENDANT ARROYO REALTY AND DEVELOPMENT, LLC's MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND. Defendant Arroyo Realty and Development, LLC's motion to vacate the default entered on May 30, 2019 is denied as moot. When Plaintiff filed the amended complaint on July 1, 2019 that made substantive changes to the original complaint, that filing opened up the default. Defendant now has a right to respond to the amended complaint. (See Ford v. Superior Court (1973) 34 Cal.App.3d 338, 342 ["A defendant who is in default as to the original complaint is entitled to service of an amended pleading making a change of substance, as opposed to a change of form, and has a right to answer or demur thereto."].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: KENT MAGNELL VS. ARROYO REALTY AND EDEVELOPMENT LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION / Notice Of Motion & Motion For Monetary Sanctions Of $13,342.00 For Bad Faith Actions And/Or Tactics; Memorandum Of Points & Authorities And Declaration Of Dennis L. Althouse, Esq. In Support Thereof
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, July 23, 2019, Line 9, DEFENDANT ARROYO REALTY AND DEVELOPMENT, LLC's Motion For Monetary Sanctions Of $13,342.00 For Bad Faith Actions And/Or Tactics. Defendant Arroyo Realty and Development, LLC's motion for sanctions is denied. While the Court does not condone attorney Hans Herb's unethical conduct, the Court cannot grant sanctions under Code of Civil Procedure § 128.5. The safe harbor provision of Code of Civil Procedure § 128.5(f)(1)(B) applies to this case because Plaintiff could have withdrawn the default prior to Defendant filing this motion. (See CPF Vaseo Associates, LLC v. Gray (2018) 29 Cal.App.5th 997, 1006 [safe harbor provision applied to motion to vacate improperly entered judgment, which could have been subject to corrective action]; Nutrition Distribution, LLC v. Southern SARMS, Inc. (2018) 20 Cal.App.5th 117, 120, 130 [21-day waiting period applies to a motion for sanctions under § 128.5 that is directed to allegedly improper actions or tactics that can be withdrawn or appropriately corrected].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ALIGN TECHNOLOG, INC. VS. ALFRED D. DENNIS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Assignment Of Rights, Restraining Order And Turnover Order
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Tuesday, July 23, 2019, Line 2, PLAINTIFF ALIGN TECHNOLOG, INC.'S Motion For Assignment Of Rights, Restraining Order And Turnover Order. Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff's post-judgment Motion for Assignment of Rights, Restraining Order and Turnover Order is unopposed and granted. Absent objection at the hearing the proposed Order will be signed. The Order shall be served by plaintiff with Notice of Entry. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@sbslawsf.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: WILL WORK INC. A MASSACHUSETTS CORPORATION VS. SANFORD TAYLOR
rulings.Calendar Matter: Appellant Will Work Inc. A Massachusetts Corporation'S Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings [For Tentative Ruling Only]
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, July 23, 2019, Line 11, Appellant Will Work Inc. A Massachusetts Corporation'S Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings [Added to Calendar for entry of Tentative Ruling Only] [Part 2 of 2]. Labor Code Section 201.9 states, "Notwithstanding subdivision (a) of Section 201, if employees are employed at a venue that hosts live theatrical or concert events and are enrolled in and routinely dispatched to employment through a hiring hall or other system of regular short-term employment established in accordance with a bona fide collective bargaining agreement these employees and their employers may establish by express terms in their collective bargaining agreement the time limits for payment of wages to an employee who is discharged or laid off." By its plain language, that is, Section 201.9 "expressly authorize[s] employers and employees to set alternative rules for the final payment of wages in a collective bargaining agreement." (Hall v. Live Nation Worldwide, Inc. (C.D. Cal. 2015) 146 F.Supp.3d 1187, 1201.) By expressly agreeing in the CBA that the facilities at which employees covered by the CBA are venues as defined by Labor Code Section 201.9 and that employees working at such venues are employed pursuant to Labor Code Section 201.9, the parties unmistakably expressed their intent that the CBA, not Sections 201 and 203, govern payment of final wages. For that additional reason, Respondent's claim is preempted. (See id. at 1202 ["If the 2015 CBA waived § 201 rights in favor of alternate final wage payment provisions, therefore, plaintiffs' right to payment exists solely as a result of the CBA and a claim based on that right is preempted"].) Under the applicable de novo standard of review (Lab. Code § 48(a)), the Labor Commissioner's contrary conclusion is not entitled to deference. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Willwork is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. [End of tentative ruling.] =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: WILL WORK INC. A MASSACHUSETTS CORPORATION VS. SANFORD TAYLOR
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, July 23, 2019, Line 11, APPELLANT WILL WORK INC. A MASSACHUSETTS CORPORATION's Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings. [Part 1 of 2]. Appellant Willwork, Inc.'s motion for judgment on the pleadings is granted without leave to amend. Respondent Sanford Taylor's claim for waiting time penalties is preempted. Section 301 of the Labor Management Relations Act (LMRA) preempts a state law claim if the claim requires the interpretation of terms in a collective bargaining agreement (CBA). (29 U.S.C. § 185; Melendez v. San Francisco Baseball Associates LLC (2019) 7 Cal.5th 1, 7-8.) Courts use a two-part test to analyze whether a state law claim is preempted, asking (1) whether the claim arises from independent state law or from the CBA and (2) if the claim arises from independent state law, whether the claim requires interpretation of the CBA or if the CBA will merely be referenced in the litigation. (Melendez, 7 Cal.5th at 9-10.) If the claim arises from the CBA or necessitates interpretation of the CBA's terms, the claim is preempted; if it arises from independent state law and only requires reference to the CBA, the claim is not preempted. (Id.) Here, Respondent's claim arises from independent state law under Labor Code Sections 201 and 203, but may also implicate Section 201.9, which allows the parties to set alternative wage timing and penalty rules in their CBA. The pivotal question, therefore, is whether Section 201.9 applies such that Respondent's claim for waiting time penalties necessitates interpretation of the CBA. Willwork relies on the following provision of the CBA to support its argument that Section 201.9 applies: "The parties recognize that the facilities at which employees covered by the Collective Bargaining Agreement work (including without limitation) are venues that host live theatrical or concert events as defined by Labor Code Section 201.9 and employees working at such venues are employed pursuant to Labor Code Section 201.9." (RJN, Ex. A, CBA Art. XV(E), at pg. 16.) The parties dispute whether that provision applies to all covered employees, or only to casual employees; Respondent also disputes whether he is properly categorized as a casual employee. Respondent's claims are preempted because "there is an active dispute over the meaning of contract terms." (Melendez, 7 Cal.5th at 10 [internal quotations omitted].) [Continued to Part 2 of 2]. =(302/EPS)
